* [Bamboo English](/wiki/Bamboo_English "Bamboo English") * [Bonin English](/wiki/Bonin_English "Bonin English") * [Hawaiian Creole](/wiki/Japanese_loanwords_in_Hawaii "Japanese loanwords in Hawaii") * *[Kyowa-go](/wiki/Kyowa-go "Kyowa-go")* * [Pseudo-Chinese](/wiki/Pseudo-Chinese "Pseudo-Chinese") * [Yilan Creole Japanese](/wiki/Yilan_Creole_Japanese "Yilan Creole Japanese") * [Yokohama Pidgin Japanese](/wiki/Yokohama_Pidgin_Japanese "Yokohama Pidgin Japanese")

**dialects** (Japanese: 北部伊豆諸島方言 *hokubu izu shoto hogen*) is a [dialect group of Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_dialects "Japanese dialects") spoken on the northernmost collection of islands in the [Izu Archipelago](/wiki/Izu_Islands "Izu Islands"), Japan. The most studied of these include the [To-shima](/wiki/To-shima,_Tokyo "To-shima, Tokyo"), [Izu Oshima](/wiki/Izu_%C5%8Cshima "Izu Ōshima") and [Miyake-jima](/wiki/Miyake-jima "Miyake-jima") dialects. These dialects have many unique traits, and can differ considerably from island to island.
Hirayama, Teruo (1965). *伊豆諸島方言の研究* (in Japanese). Meiji. pp. 153–155.

飯豊, 毅一 (1984). *講座方言学 5 関東地方の方言* (in Japanese). 国書刊行会. pp. 261–268.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Japanese dialects of the Izu Islands, Japan

| Northern Izu Archipelago dialects | |
| --- | --- |
| 北部伊豆諸島方言 | |
| Northern Izu Islands within Japan. | |
| Native to | [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") |
| Region | [Izu Islands](/wiki/Izu_Islands "Izu Islands"), [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo") |
| [Language family](/wiki/Language_family "Language family") | [Japonic](/wiki/Japonic_languages "Japonic languages")  * [Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_language "Japanese language")   + [Eastern Japanese](/wiki/Eastern_Japanese "Eastern Japanese")     - [Kantō](/wiki/Kant%C5%8D_dialect "Kantō dialect")       * **Northern Izu Archipelago dialects** |
| Dialects | * ◇ Izu Ōshima * ◇ To-shima * ◇ Nii-jima * ◇ Shikine-jima * ◇ Kōzu-shima * ◇ Miyake-jima * ◇ Mikura-jima |
| Language codes | |
| [ISO 639-3](/wiki/ISO_639-3 "ISO 639-3") | – |

The **Northern Izu Islands** **dialects** (Japanese: 北部伊豆諸島方言 *hokubu izu shoto hogen*) is a [dialect group of Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_dialects "Japanese dialects") spoken on the northernmost collection of islands in the [Izu Archipelago](/wiki/Izu_Islands "Izu Islands"), Japan. The most studied of these include the [To-shima](/wiki/To-shima,_Tokyo "To-shima, Tokyo"), [Izu Oshima](/wiki/Izu_%C5%8Cshima "Izu Ōshima") and [Miyake-jima](/wiki/Miyake-jima "Miyake-jima") dialects. These dialects have many unique traits, and can differ considerably from island to island.

## Classification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Izu_Archipelago_dialects&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Classification")]

All Northern Izu Islands dialects are part of the [Tokai-Tosan dialect group](/wiki/T%C5%8Dkai%E2%80%93T%C5%8Dsan_dialects "Tōkai–Tōsan dialects") spoken across central Japan, and are most similar to the mainland, eastern [Shizuoka dialect](/wiki/Shizuoka_dialect "Shizuoka dialect").

## General features

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Izu_Archipelago_dialects&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: General features")]

The following are general phonetic and grammatical features of the Northern Izu Archipelago dialects, with exceptions and individual variations listed in the island-specific sections.

### Phonology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Izu_Archipelago_dialects&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Phonology")]

The Northern Izu Archipelago dialects have a *chūrin* (中輪 ‘middle rim’) Tokyo standard [pitch accent](/wiki/Japanese_pitch_accent "Japanese pitch accent"). The vowel sound /e/ is somewhat narrower than in traditional Tokyo dialects, and may lose its distinction with /i/ to become [i].[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2) For example:

* *eki* (駅 *train station*) → ***i**ki*
* *kebyō* (仮病 *feigning illness*)*→ k**i**byō*
* *fude* (筆 *writing brush*) *fud**i***
* *tenki* (天気 *weather*)*→ t**i**nki*
* *sensei* (先生 *teacher*) *→ sh**i**nshē* [[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)

In some dialects, there is a /tu/ syllable. For example:

* *tsurizao* (釣り竿 *fishing rod*) *→ **tu**izao* [[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)

### Grammar

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Izu_Archipelago_dialects&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Grammar")]

Northern Izu Archipelago dialects are classified under [Eastern Japanese](/wiki/Eastern_Japanese "Eastern Japanese"), with similar [grammar](/wiki/Grammar "Grammar") structures to Shizuoka and [West Kantō dialects](/wiki/Kant%C5%8D_dialect "Kantō dialect"), but also with a strong influence from the traditional Yokohama dialect. As aforementioned, however, the differences from island to island can be extreme. Below are some notable grammatical traits.

* The conclusive auxiliary verb is the characteristic Eastern Japanese *-da* (だ).
* The connective form of *u*-ending Godan verbs experience small *tsu* (っ) insertion, whilst *su*-ending Godan verbs experience *i*-euphony. (The North Izu dialects are the furthest east this trait is found)
* *Bē* (べ) (or *bei* (べい) on To-shima) is used for persuasion and volition. *-U* (-う) is also used for volition.
* For reasons, *-kara* (から) and *-node* (ので) are generally used, with exceptions on To-shima and Mikura-jima (listed below).

## Izu Ōshima

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Izu_Archipelago_dialects&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Izu Ōshima")]

* On Izu Ōshima, pitch accent is based on the *chūrin* Tokyo standard but is not identical, rather a slight variation.
* The negative verb form is [*-nai* stem + *nai* (-ない)].
* For conjecture, *zura* (ずら) is used.
* The plain form of verbs appears as *-n* (-ん). For example) *kuru* (来る *to come*)*→ kun* (来ん)*, suru* (する *to do*)*→ shin* (しん)*, neru* (寝る *to sleep*)*→ nen* (寝ん).

## Nii-jima

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Izu_Archipelago_dialects&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Nii-jima")]

* In the older generation of Nii-jima, /e/ is lengthened.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)
* In the Nii-jima Honmura dialect, the phonemes /ti/ and /di/ are also observed.

## Mikura-jima

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Izu_Archipelago_dialects&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Mikura-jima")]

* The negative verb form is [*-nai* stem + *nee* (ねぇ)].
* For conjecture, *danbē* (だんべぇ) and *darō* (だろう) are used.

## Miyake-jima

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Izu_Archipelago_dialects&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Miyake-jima")]

* In Tsubota, /e/ tends to change to an [i], with a particularly strong tendency to do so on the syllables *ke* (け) and *re* (れ).[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[3]](#cite_note-3)
* The negative verb form is [*-nai* stem + *nee* (ねぇ) ].
* For conjecture*, zura* (ずら) and *darō* (だろう) are used.
* *-nke* (-んけ) is used as an equivalent to *kara* and *node.*

## To-shima

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Izu_Archipelago_dialects&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: To-shima")]

* The vowels in /c**e**/ and /c**o**/ are pronounced close to an *i* and *u*, respectively.[[4]](#cite_note-4)
* The diphthong /ei/ is not merged and is pronounced [ei], so that *sensei* (先生 *teacher*) is not *sensē* but *sens**ei***.[[5]](#cite_note-5)
* The negative verb form [*nai*-stem + *n* (ん) ] is used.
* For conjecture*, dan’nō* (だんのう) and *darubei* (だるべい) are used.
* *-ni* (-に) is used as an equivalent to *kara* and *node*.
* A distinction between the attributive and plain form of verbs can be seen. The attributive form ends with *-o*, whilst the plain form ends with a -*u* when particles like *bei* and *na* (な) are attached. For assertions, the verb ends with *-o*.[[6]](#cite_note-6) For example:
  + *iru* (いる *to be*)→ *iro* (いろ)
  + *neru* (寝る *to sleep*)→ *nero* (寝ろ)
  + *suru* (する *to do*)→ *shiro* (しろ)
  + *shite iru* (している *is doing*)→ *shitero* (してろ)
  + *kaku* (書く *to write*)→ *kako* (書こ).
* The Western Japanese past negative form *-zatta* (-ざった *was not*) is found.

## References

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Izu_Archipelago_dialects&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: References")]

2. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-:1_2-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-:1_2-1) [***c***](#cite_ref-:1_2-2) Hirayama, Teruo (1965). *伊豆諸島方言の研究* (in Japanese). Meiji. pp. 87–89.
3. **[^](#cite_ref-3)** Hirayama, Teruo (1965). *伊豆諸島方言の研究* (in Japanese). Meiji. pp. 153–155.
4. **[^](#cite_ref-4)** 柴田, 武 (1988). *方言論* (in Japanese). 平凡社. pp. 284–285.
5. **[^](#cite_ref-5)** 飯豊, 毅一 (1984). *講座方言学 5 関東地方の方言* (in Japanese). 国書刊行会. p. 244.
6. **[^](#cite_ref-6)** 飯豊, 毅一 (1984). *講座方言学 5 関東地方の方言* (in Japanese). 国書刊行会. pp. 261–268.
